planting.note: Start seeds indoors after 4–6 weeks cold-moist stratification (refrigerator at 40°F) or direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; transplant into well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun after last frost.

directions: Start seeds indoors after 4–6 weeks cold-moist stratification (refrigerator at 40°F) or direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; transplant into well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun after last frost. Echinacea angustifolia is the coneflower of the native short-grass and mixed-grass prairies of the Great Plains, shorter and more compact than E. purpurea with narrow, lance-shaped, hairy leaves and pale pink to rose-purple flowers with horizontal to reflexed ray petals; it is regarded by herbalists as the most potent of the medicinal echinaceas for immunostimulant use, and the root was one of the most widely used medicinal plants of Great Plains indigenous peoples. Drought-tolerant, long-lived, and difficult to establish from seed — purchase transplants for fastest results.

planting.note: Start seeds after cold-moist stratification for 4–8 weeks, or direct sow in fall for natural stratification, in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in full sun.

directions: Start seeds after cold-moist stratification for 4–8 weeks, or direct sow in fall for natural stratification, in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in full sun. Echinacea pallida is a midwestern prairie native easily distinguished from other coneflowers by its unusually long (up to 4 inches), very narrow, drooping, pale-pink to pale-mauve ray petals that give the flower a distinctive "swept back" or "mop-headed" appearance unlike any other coneflower; it is native to dry to mesic prairies from Michigan and Tennessee west to Nebraska and is an important source of late-spring to early-summer nectar for bumblebees and specialist coneflower bees. The root is used medicinally like other echinaceas; it is somewhat more floriferous and heat-tolerant than E. angustifolia and easier to establish in garden conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in very well-drained, rocky or sandy, dry to moderately dry soil in full sun; cold stratification over winter in the ground produces reliable germination.

directions: Direct sow in fall in very well-drained, rocky or sandy, dry to moderately dry soil in full sun; cold stratification over winter in the ground produces reliable germination. Echinacea atrorubens is one of the most vividly colored of the true species coneflowers, native to dry limestone and rocky prairies of Kansas, Oklahoma, and Texas, with dark red-crimson to deep magenta ray petals that are much more intensely saturated than typical pink species; it is adapted to alkaline, rocky, dry soils in the south-central prairie region and blooms in June–July. A species for true prairie garden enthusiasts in zones 5–9, it is excellent in dry prairie plantings, gravel gardens, and xeriscape designs in its native range; plants develop a deep taproot and are extremely drought-tolerant once established.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic, dry to moderately dry soil in full sun; sanguine coneflower is native to the sandy, acidic soils of the coastal plain and pineland habitats of eastern Texas, Louisiana, Arkansas, and Oklahoma.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic, dry to moderately dry soil in full sun; sanguine coneflower is native to the sandy, acidic soils of the coastal plain and pineland habitats of eastern Texas, Louisiana, Arkansas, and Oklahoma. Echinacea sanguinea is the coneflower of the southeastern coastal plain pineland habitats, producing narrow, strongly reflexed, rose-red to blood-red ray petals and a tall, conical, brownish-orange central disk; its tolerance of sandy, acidic soils and the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast region makes it the best-adapted true-species echinacea for zone 7–9 southern gardens. It is rare in cultivation but increasingly available from native plant nurseries in the South, and is important for ecological gardens in its native range where it supports specialist native bees.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in extremely well-drained, dry, alkaline, thin cedar-glade soil in full sun; Tennessee coneflower is adapted to the thin, rocky, alkaline cedar-glade soils of the Nashville Basin and will struggle in heavy, wet, or acidic soils — ensure sharp drainage and do not amend with acidifying materials.

directions: Direct sow in fall in extremely well-drained, dry, alkaline, thin cedar-glade soil in full sun; Tennessee coneflower is adapted to the thin, rocky, alkaline cedar-glade soils of the Nashville Basin and will struggle in heavy, wet, or acidic soils — ensure sharp drainage and do not amend with acidifying materials. Echinacea tennesseensis was listed as a federally endangered species in 1979 with only six remaining natural populations in the cedar glades of central Tennessee; it was delisted in 2011 following successful conservation and propagation efforts, making it one of the few plants successfully recovered from the endangered list. It is distinctive among echinaceas for its ray petals that angle slightly upward rather than drooping, giving it a more rounded, dahlia-like appearance; grow it in alkaline, very well-drained, dry conditions as a living symbol of successful plant conservation.
sources: US Fish and Wildlife Service Recovery Plan
